content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Paul Graham wrote the rulebook for protecting deep work in 2009. AI agents just rewrote half the rules — here’s the new shape of a builder’s day.
I shipped more last month than I have in any month of my career. I also felt, for the first time in years, vaguely scatterbrained at the end of most days — like I’d been busy without being deep. Both things were true at once, and that contradiction is the whole story.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

For fifteen years, the operating manual for engineers who wanted to do real work was simple: protect long, unbroken blocks of time, treat meetings as the enemy, and guard your attention like it’s the scarce resource it is. That manual still works. But it was written for a world where the bottleneck was you typing. That’s no longer the only bottleneck — and in some cases, it’s not the bottleneck at all.
This is a playbook for the part nobody warned you about: what happens to deep work when half your day is spent directing machines that work while you don’t.

The framework everyone quotes and half-remembers
In July 2009, Paul Graham published a short essay called “Maker’s Schedule, Manager’s Schedule.” If you’ve worked in software, you’ve absorbed its argument whether or not you’ve read it.
His claim: there are two fundamentally different ways to use time. Managers run on the manager’s schedule — the day chopped into one-hour boxes, each slot interchangeable, a meeting dropped anywhere costing roughly an hour. Makers run on the maker’s schedule — they need time in units of at least half a day, because building something nontrivial requires holding a complex system in your head, and that takes a long ramp.
The killer line, the one everyone remembers: for someone on the maker’s schedule, a single meeting in the middle of the afternoon doesn’t cost an hour. It splits the day into two fragments, each too small to do anything hard in. That’s the real tax. Not the meeting — the fragmentation.
Graham’s prescription was to protect the blocks and quarantine the interruptions — his own fix was pushing founder meetings to the end of the day, where they couldn’t shatter a morning of building.
Fifteen years later, that prescription is still correct. It’s just incomplete.

What deep work actually is — and why it’s getting more valuable, not less
Cal Newport gave the protected-block idea its sharpest definition in his 2016 book Deep Work. He calls it professional activity performed in a state of distraction-free concentration that pushes your cognitive capabilities to their limit — work that creates new value, improves your skill, and is hard to replicate.
He contrasts it with shallow work: the logistical, non-cognitively-demanding stuff you can do while distracted, which tends not to create new value and is easy to replicate.
Here’s the uncomfortable part for engineers in 2026. AI is extremely good at the easy-to-replicate, do-it-while-distracted layer. Boilerplate, glue code, the first draft of a test suite, the obvious refactor — that’s exactly the work getting automated. What’s left, what only you can do, is the part that was always the hard part: the spec, the architecture, the judgment call about whether the agent’s output is actually right.
Newport’s framing didn’t get less relevant in the AI era. It got more relevant, because the proportion of your day that is genuinely deep work — and nothing else — is going up.

The number every builder should tattoo somewhere
In July 2025, METR — a nonprofit AI evaluation lab — published a randomized controlled trial that should be required reading before anyone declares AI a productivity miracle.
They took 16 experienced open-source developers working in repositories they knew intimately (averaging 22,000+ stars, over a million lines of code), gave them 246 real issues from their own projects, and randomly assigned each issue to allow or disallow AI tools. The AI tools were the frontier ones — primarily Cursor Pro with Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet at the time.
The result: when developers used AI, they took 19% longer to finish.
That’s not the part that should haunt you. This is: those same developers predicted AI would make them 24% faster. And even after living through the slowdown, they still believed AI had sped them up by about 20%. They were wrong by roughly 40 percentage points about their own productivity, in real time, on their own code.
I want to be precise about what this study does and doesn’t say, because it’s been wildly over-generalized. METR themselves stress it’s a snapshot — 16 expert developers, mature codebases they knew cold, early-2025 tooling. They explicitly do not claim AI fails to speed up most developers, or developers in unfamiliar code, or any domain outside software. And critically: METR published a follow-up in 2026 with newer data on later tools, with different results — the early number is a moment in time, not a law of nature.
But the perception gap — the part where smart people felt fast while being slow — is the finding that travels. That’s not about model quality. That’s about how the feeling of productivity got decoupled from the fact of it.

Why AI feels fast even when it isn’t
The feeling is real, and it’s seductive. When an agent generates 200 lines while you watch, your brain registers progress. The screen fills up. Something is happening. You are, in the most literal sense, producing more output per minute of typing.
But the clock that matters isn’t typing time — it’s task-completion time, start to finish. And that clock includes everything the demo doesn’t show you: reading what the agent wrote, finding the subtle thing it got wrong, re-prompting, waiting, re-reading, deciding whether to fix it or rip it out and do it yourself.
The work didn’t disappear. It moved. It moved from your fingers to your eyes and your judgment. And eyes-and-judgment work is harder to feel good about, because it doesn’t fill the screen.
This is the trap the playbook has to defend against: optimizing for the feeling of velocity instead of the fact of shipping.

The new fragmentation: babysitting agents
Now connect this back to Graham. His enemy was the meeting — the scheduled interruption that splits your maker’s day. In 2026, you have a new source of fragmentation, and it’s worse because it’s self-inflicted and feels like work.
Call it agent-babysitting. You kick off an agent on a task. It’ll take eight minutes. So you… check Slack. Glance at the other agent. Tweak a prompt. Read the half-finished output. Context-switch back. Forget where you were. Re-read your own code to remember.
Gloria Mark’s research at UC Irvine — the source of the widely cited figure — found it takes an average of about 23 minutes to fully return to a task after an interruption. Her later work documented that knowledge workers now switch tasks far more often than that recovery time allows, meaning many of us never reach full cognitive recovery before the next switch arrives.
An eight-minute agent run that you “fill” with three context-switches doesn’t save you eight minutes. It can cost you the next hour of depth. Every agent you babysit is a meeting you scheduled with yourself, every few minutes, all day.
That’s the precise mechanism behind “felt productive, was 19% slower.” It’s Graham’s fragmentation problem wearing a new costume.

The new shape of a maker’s day
So here’s what I’ve actually changed. Not theory — the structure I run now.
The old maker’s day was a battle for two unbroken 3–4 hour blocks, defended against meetings. That’s still the spine. But inside it, the work has changed shape. Less of the block is you typing the implementation. More of it is spec, direction, and review — the parts AI can’t own.
The new day has three distinct modes, and the entire skill is in not blending them:
1. Deep maker blocks (protected, no agents running). For the work that requires your full system-in-head concentration: the hard architecture, the gnarly bug, the design you have to think through. During these, agents are off. No babysitting. This is Graham’s and Newport’s block, fully intact — and it’s more valuable than ever, because it’s now the rarest mode.
2. Direction-and-review blocks (agents running, you supervising in batch). This is the genuinely new mode. You write a tight spec, dispatch one or more agents, and then — this is the whole trick — you do not hover. You batch. You let three agents run, go do a real chunk of review or your own work, and come back to evaluate all of them at once. The mental model is a tech lead reviewing three pull requests in a sitting, not a parent watching three toddlers near a pool.
3. Shallow/admin blocks (everything else). Email, planning, the logistics. Where it always went. Don’t let it leak into mode 1.
The failure mode — the thing that produces the 19% — is collapsing mode 2 into mode 1. Trying to do deep work while babysitting agents. You get neither. You get the feeling of both.

The batching framework
Here’s the concrete operating rule I use to keep agent supervision from shredding my day. Steal it.
Batch by checkpoint, not by impulse. Decide in advance when you’ll look at agent output — at natural breakpoints, not every time you feel a twitch of curiosity. The default should be: dispatch, walk away, return at the checkpoint.
One deep thing OR many shallow things — never deep-plus-supervision. During a protected maker block, run zero agents. During a supervision block, don’t pretend you’re also doing deep work. Pick a lane.
Spec quality is the new typing speed. The single highest-leverage skill is writing a spec so clear the agent can’t misinterpret it — because a vague prompt guarantees a babysitting spiral of re-prompt, re-read, re-fix. Time spent sharpening the spec is the cheapest time in the whole loop.
Review is real work, so schedule it like work. Reviewing agent output is cognitively demanding — it’s literally judgment under uncertainty. Treat it as a deep-ish task that deserves a block, not something you squeeze between two other things while half-watching a progress bar.
Measure the right clock. At the end of a week, the only question that matters is: did the thing ship, and was it right? Not did I feel fast. The METR developers prove your gut is an unreliable narrator here. Trust the ship log, not the vibe.

The honest read
Let me hold myself to the same standard I’d want from anyone selling a productivity framework.
This is one model of work, not the model. Graham himself said the maker/manager split is a simplification. Plenty of valuable engineering — incident response, pairing, mentoring, the messy human coordination of shipping real software — doesn’t fit a clean block diagram, and never did.
It’s deeply role-dependent. A staff engineer architecting systems and a junior developer learning a codebase live in different worlds. METR’s own caveat is the sharpest one: their slowdown showed up among experts in code they knew cold. The study’s authors note AI may well speed up less experienced developers, or anyone working in unfamiliar territory. If you’re learning, the calculus is different — the agent might be your fastest teacher even when it’s not your fastest typist.
The numbers are snapshots, not constants. The 19% is from early-2025 tools, in one narrow setting, with 16 developers. METR has since published newer data showing different results as tooling improved. Don’t carry “AI makes you 19% slower” around as a fact about 2026 — carry the mechanism: that perceived speed and actual speed can diverge, hard, and that fragmentation is the hidden cost.
There is no silver bullet here. Batching agent supervision won’t fix a broken team, an incoherent roadmap, or a codebase nobody understands. It’s a discipline for protecting attention, not a substitute for the things attention is supposed to be spent on.
And the deepest caveat: the productivity-perception trap doesn’t go away because you read about it. Knowing the feeling
